title: Routing Queries to Tools
concept: How a router determines which tool matches the agent's intent, using classification or embeddings.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: when an agent fills in a tool's arguments (like the search query or file path), where do those values come from?

options.text: The model generates them based on the current situation and goal
They are hard-coded and never change
The user must type every value by hand each time

exercises.explain: Dynamic parameter binding means the model fills in argument values (query, path, code) on the fly, based on the current environment and what the task needs right now.

cards.title: Which door does the request go through?
cards.body: An agent might have 20 tools: search, calculator, calendar, code runner. When a request comes in, something has to pick the right one. That picker is called a router.

cards.title: What a router does
cards.body: A router reads the user's request and decides which tool best matches the intent. It's like a traffic controller sending each request down the right lane.

cards.title: Hospital front desk
cards.body: You walk into a hospital and say 'my tooth hurts.' The front desk doesn't treat you, it routes you to the dentist. A router does the same: it doesn't answer, it sends you to the right specialist tool.

cards.title: Two requests, two lanes
cards.body: 'What's 15% of 240?' routes to the calculator. 'Book a meeting Friday' routes to the calendar. Same agent, but the router matched each request to a different tool.

cards.title: Myth: the router answers the question
cards.body: Nope! The router only chooses which tool to use. The tool does the actual work. The router's whole job is matching, not solving.

cards.title: Two ways to route
cards.body: Routers pick tools in two common ways: by classifying the request into a labeled category, or by comparing meanings with embeddings.

left.text: Sorts the request into a fixed set of labels, like 'math' or 'calendar'.

right.text: Turns the request into a meaning-vector and picks the tool whose description is closest.

exercises.prompt: A user asks 'Add lunch with Sam to my schedule tomorrow.' What should the router do?

options.text: Route the request to the calendar tool
Answer the question itself without using any tool
Route it to the calculator because it mentions a number
Refuse because it can't tell what the user wants

exercises.explain: The intent is scheduling an event, so the router matches it to the calendar tool. The router's job is matching intent to the right specialist, not answering itself.

exercises.prompt: Embedding-based routing works by comparing the meaning of the request to the meaning of each tool's description and picking the closest match.
exercises.explain: Embeddings turn text into meaning-vectors. The router picks the tool whose description is closest in meaning to the request, even if the exact words differ.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the right word.
exercises.sentence: A router does not solve the request; it matches the request's intent to the best tool.
exercises.explain: A router matches the request's intent, what the user is trying to do, to the tool most likely to handle it.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation of a router sounds AI-generated and overly generic?

options.text: A router is a sophisticated component that intelligently orchestrates optimal tool selection to seamlessly deliver superior outcomes across diverse scenarios.
A router reads the request, guesses which tool fits, and hands it off, like a front desk sending you to the right room.
If you ask for the weather, the router sends you to the weather tool instead of the calculator.

exercises.explain: The first option is stuffed with vague buzzwords ('seamlessly', 'superior outcomes') and no concrete example, a classic sign of empty AI-generated filler.

exercises.prompt: An agent has a search tool and a calculator. A user types: 'How much is a 20% tip on $85?' The router uses embeddings but the request never says the word 'calculator.' How does it still pick the right tool?

options.text: It matches by meaning: the request's meaning-vector is closest to the calculator's description, even without matching words
It can't route because the word 'calculator' is missing from the request
It always defaults to the search tool when unsure
It asks the user to name the tool before doing anything

exercises.explain: Embedding routing compares MEANING, not exact words. '20% tip on $85' is clearly a math task, so its meaning-vector lands closest to the calculator's description and gets routed there. That's the power of dynamic tool selection: the router matches intent to the right tool, whether by classifying into labels or by comparing meaning-vectors, so the agent uses the perfect specialist for each request.
